front: Painting: an incubus crouches on the chest of a sleeping woman while a pale-eyed horse pokes its head through red curtains
Painting: Nicolai Abildgaard reworked this piece to show two people instead of one
Painting: widely popularized by an engraving by Thomas Burke
Painting: a small table in this work holds a book, a mirror, and a vial
Painting: a later version added an erotic sculpture of a man and woman to a table that previously held only a mirror, book, and vial
Painting: an unfinished portrait of Anna Landolt, the niece of Johann Caspar Lavater, appears on the back of this canvas
Painting: Doja Cat recreated this work for her 2024 Scarlet tour posters, depicting herself as the central figure
Painting: a nude god stands on the prow of a boat pulling a chained sea serpent while Hymir cowers; the artist's Royal Academy diploma work
back: Thor Battering the Midgard Serpent (1790) (Henry Fuseli)

front: Painting: inspired by Thomas Percy's translation of the Northern Antiquities

front: Painting: a spray of blood is visible from the chained creature being yanked into the prow of a boat by a nude god

Milton Gallery / The Shepherd's Dream (Henry Fuseli)

front: Work: commercially failed gallery of 47 paintings based on Paradise Lost, produced over an eight-year period
Painting: from the Milton Gallery; a floating throng of fairies bewitch a peasant, drawing on Paradise Lost's comparison of fallen angels to fairies
Artist: principal patron who rescued Fuseli from financial ruin after the Milton Gallery's public failure
Work: venture Fuseli contributed to alongside Angelica Kauffmann before launching his own Milton project
work: Shakespeare-Inspired Works (Titania and Bottom, Lady Macbeth Seizing the Daggers, Lady Macbeth Sleepwalking)

front: Painting: a tiny man with outstretched arms stands in the palm of a donkey-headed figure surrounded by an eerie fairy retinue, including a girl with a butterfly for hair

front: Painting: the ghostly title figure raises her finger to her lips to hush a man yielding his weapons

work: Shakespeare-Inspired Works (Titania and Bottom, Lady Macbeth Seizing the Daggers, Lady Macbeth Sleepwalking)

front: Painting: nightmarish Fuseli scene based on Shakespeare's Henry VIII
back: The Vision of Catherine of Aragon (Henry Fuseli)
work: Shakespeare-Inspired Works (Titania and Bottom, Lady Macbeth Seizing the Daggers, Lady Macbeth Sleepwalking)

front: Painting: draws on Swiss mythology; inspired by Jacques-Louis David's similar oath-swearing scene with the Horatii
Artist: Swiss-born Romantic painter (Johann Heinrich Fussli) who translated Lavater's Essays on Physiognomy and Winckelmann's Reflections on the Painting and Sculpture of the Greeks
Artist: studied alongside Jakob Bodmer, who appears in a self-portrait in which the artist is seen talking to him next to a bust of Homer
Artist: became a professor at the Royal Academy in 1790 instead of Joseph Bonomi, prompting Sir Joshua Reynolds to resign in protest
Artist: a 'wizard painter' rumored to fuel his imagination with raw pork and opium; Baron Brooke Boothby threw a forested Gothic pageant for him
Artist: painter eulogized by William Blake as 'both Turk and Jew'; paired with Blake in a Tate Britain exhibition
Artist: former Somerset House resident whose wife, Sophia Rawlings, was the subject of naked drawings later exhibited there
Artist: served as both translator and cover designer for Lavater's Aphorisms on Man
Painting: a ghostly figure sits cross-legged with its head hidden in its long hair, which falls into its lap
Painting: bonneted crones extend their left arms forward in this Macbeth-inspired work
Painting: a bug-eyed woman in a red-ribboned bonnet sits on a rock as her black cape is blown by the wind
Painting: a star-crowned cherub with a torch hovers above a young girl being carried to heaven by an angel
back: The Apotheosis of Penelope Boothby (Henry Fuseli)

front: Painting: Fuseli's homage to Burgundian legend
Painting: Fuseli's mythological depiction of a hero navigating between a sea monster and a whirlpool
back: Odysseus in Front of Scylla and Charybdis (Henry Fuseli)
